attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman is being reviewed on the surgical ward 36 hours after an elective total hip replacement. The duty intern has been asked to see her because she has developed a bothersome cough. She reports feeling generally unwell but denies any specific pain beyond her surgical site. She has no significant medical history. Her medications include aspirin 100 mg daily for venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis, and she is using sequential compression devices (SCDs). She takes no other regular medications. She smokes five cigarettes per day, with a 12-pack-year smoking history.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable due to the cough but is otherwise stable. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 130/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, temperature 37.4°C, and SpO2 95% on room air. Her surgical wound appears clean and dry. Chest auscultation reveals decreased breath sounds at the left base with no crepitations or wheeze audible. Investigations including an ECG, full blood examination and electrolytes, urea and creatinine are normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's cough?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely cause of this patient's cough is atelectasis. Atelectasis is a common postoperative complication, typically occurring within 24-48 hours after surgery. This patient's presentation is consistent with atelectasis: cough developing 36 hours postoperatively and decreased breath sounds at the lung base. The normal investigations also support this diagnosis, as atelectasis typically does not cause significant changes in blood tests or ECG.

It is important to note that while atelectasis has traditionally been associated with postoperative fever, numerous studies have failed to show a consistent association between atelectasis and fever in post-operative patients. Thus, for postoperative patients with symptoms of atelectasis and fever, consideration of other contributing factors is important, and a fever should not be automatically attributed to atelectasis.

Pulmonary embolism is an important consideration in postoperative patients, especially following orthopaedic surgery. However, pulmonary embolism typically presents with more significant respiratory symptoms, tachycardia, and often pleuritic chest pain, which are not prominent in this case. Additionally, the patient is on VTE prophylaxis with aspirin and SCDs, which reduces the likelihood of pulmonary embolism.

Medication-related cough is possible but less likely in this scenario. While drug-induced cough can occur, it is not commonly associated with aspirin, which is the only new medication mentioned.

Community-acquired pneumonia is a possibility, but it usually develops later in the postoperative period (after 72 hours) and would typically present with more pronounced respiratory symptoms and potentially productive cough. The absence of crepitations on auscultation also makes pneumonia less likely.

Aspiration pneumonitis can occur postoperatively, especially in patients who have had general anaesthesia. However, it typically presents more acutely, often within hours of the procedure, and would usually be associated with more severe symptoms and abnormal chest findings.

The management of atelectasis involves encouraging deep breathing exercises, early mobilisation, and the use of incentive spirometry. These interventions help to re-expand collapsed alveoli, improve oxygenation, and prevent further complications. In this case, addressing the patient's pain management to facilitate mobilisation and encouraging deep breathing exercises would be key parts of the treatment plan.
learningPoints: Atelectasis is a common postoperative complication, often presenting with cough within 24-48 hours after surgery.
Assessment of postoperative cough should include consideration of atelectasis, pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, and aspiration.
Atelectasis often does not present with fever, contrary to traditional teaching; postoperative fever should prompt consideration of other causes.
references: Zeng C, Lagier D, Lee JW, Vidal Melo MF. Perioperative Pulmonary Atelectasis: Part I. Biology and Mechanisms. Anesthesiology. 2022 Jan 1;136(1):181-205. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9869183/

Lagier D, Zeng C, Fernandez-Bustamante A, Vidal Melo MF. Perioperative Pulmonary Atelectasis: Part II. Clinical Implications. Anesthesiology. 2022 Jan 1;136(1):206-236. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9885487/

Mavros MN, Velmahos GC, Falagas ME. Atelectasis as a cause of postoperative fever: where is the clinical evidence? Chest. 2011 Aug;140(2):418-424. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21527508/

Crompton JG, Crompton PD, Matzinger P. Does Atelectasis Cause Fever After Surgery? Putting a Damper on Dogma. JAMA Surg. 2019;154(5):375–376. Available at: https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamasurgery/article-abstract/2727130

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
